# Episode Summary Style Guide — v1.2
**Last updated:** June 2026
**Applies to:** `summary` field in `episode_meta.json`, generated by `tools/generate-episode-intel.js`

---

## What this is

The episode summary is not a recap. It is an editorial judgement written by someone who listened and has something to say. The purpose is to help someone decide in 30 seconds whether 3 hours is worth their evening.

---

## Three styles — AI picks the best fit

There are three summary styles. The model reads the episode's concepts and selects whichever style will produce the most distinctive, fresh-sounding result for that episode. The chosen style is stored in `summary_style` (A, B, or C). Variety across the library matters — the same style should never dominate.

**When to use which:**
- **Style A** — episodes with a clear intellectual arc or a guest whose personality drives the conversation
- **Style B** — episodes built around a single provocative premise or a counterintuitive thesis that runs throughout
- **Style C** — episodes where the real subject is bigger or stranger than the stated topic — where zooming out reveals something wry or unexpected
- **Style D** — episodes where a clear thesis invites genuine pushback; the summary is written from the perspective of a smart skeptic who came in unconvinced and left shifted

---

## Universal rules (apply to all styles)

- 110–125 words total. Count before finalising. Hard limit.
- Three paragraphs separated by blank lines.
- First names only after first mention. Never full last names mid-summary.
- Never start with a host or guest name as the literal first word.
- Never "In this episode..."
- Maximum ONE colon in the entire summary.
- **BANNED everywhere:** em dashes, "It's not X it's Y" and all variations, the word "tension" (it has its own field), "dives deep / unpacks / explores / breaks down / sits down with", fascinating / insightful / thought-provoking / compelling / powerful, "framework", "checklist".
- **NO triads:** three adjectives, three parallel clauses, three "why" / "into" / "that" phrases in a row. Two is fine. Three is banned.

---

## Style A — "Opinionated Friend"

P1 (~35 words): What the episode is doing or refusing to do. Open with attitude, not introduction.

P2 (~60 words): The arc as movement. Something starts, shifts, lands differently. Not a topic list — show the shape.

P3 (~25 words): Exactly THREE short declarative sentences. None the same length. None the same structure. Ends on a feeling or question, never a recap.

**Worked example — Episode 505 — Chris Williamson + Mark Manson**

> Mark Manson and Chris spend two hours refusing to be encouraging. The topics are familiar — confidence, blame, the relationships you settle for — but the framing is not: these aren't things you need to learn. They're things you already know and keep finding reasons to ignore.
>
> The conversation starts personal and gradually pulls back to something bigger. Modern life, they argue, removed all the friction that used to make things feel like they mattered. Nobody warned you that would happen. Nobody warned you that ease was part of the problem.
>
> You will recognise yourself in at least six of these. Probably more. Some of them will stay uncomfortable past the episode.

*(101 words)*

---

## Style B — "Premise / Texture / Effect"

P1 (~35 words): Open on the episode's central premise stated as a provocation. Often riffs on or subverts the episode title. Makes the reader do a small double-take.

P2 (~65 words): Pick ONE specific concrete detail from the concepts and let it carry the paragraph. Then describe the dynamic of the conversation — how it moves, what it keeps doing — rather than listing topics. The detail used in P2 must NOT be the sharpest_line. Use a different one.

P3 (~20 words): Exactly TWO sentences. The effect the episode leaves on the listener. Both short. Both land hard. Not a summary of content.

---

## Style C — "The Long Zoom"

Find the single most counterintuitive or wry observation running underneath the whole episode — not as a topic intro, but as a thesis about the world. The episode arrives as evidence, not as the subject. Tone is allowed to be playful, amused, slightly absurd. One continuous arc. No topic list.

P1 (~30 words): A bold, unexpected claim about the world. Not about the episode. Should make the reader blink.

P2 (~70 words): The episode provides the evidence. Specific, concrete, allowed to be wry. Show how the conversation proves the opening claim. Do NOT repeat the sharpest_line as the example here.

P3 (~20 words): The turn. An implication, a question, or a kicker that reframes everything above. Ends sharp. One or two sentences.

**Worked example — Episode 518 — Rory Sutherland + Tom Goodwin (Uncensored CMO)**

> Every organization eventually becomes a machine for killing its own best ideas. The only question is how fast.
>
> Rory Sutherland and Tom Goodwin spend the episode providing evidence. Process worship kills serendipity, which everyone suspects but nobody can measure. Attribution fraud lets ad platforms claim credit for sales that were already happening. Together they describe a system that has optimized itself past the point where interesting things can survive, confusing the flat part of the adoption curve with proof that something does not work.
>
> Which raises the question nobody in your organization wants to answer: what would you greenlight if you could not justify it with data?

*(112 words)*

---

## Voice (all styles)

Opinionated but not smug. The writer has a take, not a verdict. Think: a friend who just finished listening and is telling you whether to bother. Not neutral. Not promotional. Not trying to cover everything — pointing at the one thing that made it land.

---

## THE benchmark — Style C at its best

**Episode 503 — "AI Will Make Plumbers Earn More Than Lawyers!" — Steven Bartlett + Daniel Priestley**

This is the reference output. When in doubt about what Style C should feel like, come back here.

> For thirty years, society told ambitious people to avoid working with their hands. That advice is now worth about as much as a law degree.
>
> Priestley's clearest example is plumbing. Tasks done on screens automate first and automate completely. Tasks requiring physical presence, improvisation, and judgment at 2 a.m. do not. Supply of tradespeople collapsed as everyone chased degrees. Demand stayed constant. The premium for showing up in person with skills that cannot be delegated to a subscription is now rising faster than anything the knowledge economy produces.
>
> The people who skipped university to learn a trade were not falling behind. They were waiting for the rest of us to arrive.

*(119 words)*

What makes it work: P1 opens on a bold claim about the world (not the episode), P2 uses ONE detail (plumbing) to prove the claim without listing anything else, P3 lands with a twist that reframes everything above in two short sentences.

---

## Style D — "The Skeptic" (button: "Surprise Me")

Find the strongest objection a reasonable person brings into this episode — not a strawman, the actual best counter-argument. P2 shows the ONE thing from the episode that made that objection harder to hold. P3 lands where a smart skeptic actually ends up: not converted, but moved.

P1 (~30w): The strongest objection as a statement. Makes skeptical readers feel seen. Should make a true believer slightly uncomfortable.

P2 (~65w): ONE specific thing from the episode that cracked the objection. Concrete. Must NOT be the sharpest_line. Show what moved the needle, not the whole argument.

P3 (~20w): EXACTLY 2 sentences. Where the skeptic lands. Honest — not a conversion, not full rejection. Ends sharp.

**Worked example — Episode 519 — Chris Williamson + Arthur Brooks**

> The premise sounds like something printed on a motivational calendar: mid-life doesn't have to mean decline, it's actually a transition. You have heard this before, usually from people who needed it to be true.
>
> Brooks has one piece of evidence that doesn't come from self-help. Fluid intelligence, the processing speed that built your career, peaks around forty and starts declining. That part is not opinion. What he adds is that crystallized intelligence, built from experience and judgment, keeps growing, but only if you stop pretending the first kind isn't fading. The honesty of the diagnosis is what makes the rest feel less like a pep talk.
>
> You don't leave convinced the second half is better. You leave less convinced it's worse.

---

## Version history

v1.0 (June 2026) — Initial. One style (Opinionated Friend). Bans: em-dashes, triads, "It's not X it's Y", word bans, plot-recap P2s.

v1.1 (June 2026) — Three styles locked: A (Opinionated Friend), B (Premise/Texture/Effect), C (The Long Zoom). AI selects best fit per episode. `summary_style` field added to `episode_meta.json` schema.

v1.2 (June 2026) — Style D added: The Skeptic. Button label "Surprise Me". Style B renamed to "One Premise" on UI buttons. Intel generation panel added to extract.html with per-style shortcut buttons.
